content.text: Branching logic at the prompt layer — selecting, assembling, or parameterising prompts at runtime based on observable conditions — is the mechanism that turns a linear LLM pipeline into an adaptive system capable of qualitatively different behaviour for different inputs, without retraining the model.

Conditional prompting is the practice of making the prompt that reaches a language model depend on a condition evaluated at runtime. Rather than sending the same fixed system prompt to every request, the pipeline first
the current context — classifying intent, inspecting prior outputs, or reading external state — and then
to the prompt template that best fits that context.
Within prompt chaining (the broader discipline of composing sequences of LLM calls), conditional prompting is what adds

. A chain without conditionals is a straight pipeline: every input traverses the same fixed sequence of prompts. Conditional prompting turns that pipeline into a directed graph where the path taken depends on the data — exactly what enables agentic systems to plan, self-correct, and adapt mid-execution.
The concept maps directly to the

construct in code. The key difference is that the condition itself can be evaluated by an LLM (a lightweight classification call) rather than written as a deterministic boolean predicate, which makes the approach applicable to unstructured natural-language inputs where rule-based parsing would be brittle.

A single monolithic system prompt cannot simultaneously be optimal for every possible input. A support assistant prompt tuned for billing queries degrades on technical debugging. A creative writing assistant prompt fails on structured data extraction. Trying to cover every case in one prompt produces
: long, conflicting instructions that cause instruction-following quality to drop and token costs to rise.

The alternative — maintaining entirely separate deployments for each use case — multiplies infrastructure overhead and breaks any possibility of shared context. Conditional prompting threads the needle: a single model receives different, purpose-built prompts depending on the request, keeping each prompt focused while sharing the same inference infrastructure and conversation history.

At scale, even a two-way conditional split can eliminate 30–50% of input tokens per request by ensuring each branch carries only the instructions relevant to its use case. That cost reduction compounds across high-traffic systems.
Each branch contains only instructions for its use case. Shorter, focused prompts yield higher instruction-following accuracy and reduce per-request token cost.
Each prompt template is a separate versioned artefact. Improving the billing branch cannot regress the technical branch; A/B tests are naturally scoped.
Routing simple or short requests to a cheaper, smaller model while reserving powerful models for complex branches can cut inference cost by 40–70% in mixed-workload pipelines.
The routing decision is an explicit, loggable step. Debugging why the system behaved a certain way means inspecting the evaluator output, not reverse-engineering a monolithic prompt.
Branches can themselves contain further conditional branches, building arbitrarily sophisticated behaviour from simple, auditable units.
A well-designed conditional flow always has a fallback branch. When no condition matches, the system asks a clarifying question rather than hallucinating a response into an ill-fitted template.

If the evaluator misclassifies an input, the wrong template runs. A routing error can produce confidently wrong outputs in the right tone — potentially worse than a generic response from a single prompt.
Each branch is another prompt to test, version, and monitor. For small projects or early prototypes, this overhead can outweigh the benefit.
An LLM-based evaluator adds a full round-trip — typically 200 ms–1 s — before the main generation step begins.

Conditional systems make a closed-world assumption: inputs are classified into known categories. Inputs outside all defined categories degrade to the fallback, which may disappoint if the fallback is under-designed.
logical paths to cover in a test suite.
A conditional prompt pipeline has two distinct logical phases:
The evaluator receives the current context (user message, prior chain state, retrieved documents) and produces a structured signal that drives routing. Three common evaluator implementations exist:
Regex or keyword matching. Zero LLM cost, deterministic, but brittle for natural-language paraphrases.
A fast, cheap LLM call with a constrained output format ("reply with exactly one word"). Flexible and handles paraphrases, but adds latency and a non-zero error rate.
Compute the input embedding and compare cosine similarity against labelled prototype embeddings. No LLM round-trip, but requires a curated prototype library.
Once the evaluator produces a route label, the dispatcher selects the matching template, injects runtime variables, and submits the assembled prompt. The formal dispatch is:
When the evaluator outputs a confidence score alongside its classification, the routing rule incorporates a threshold

higher (e.g. 0.9) makes the system conservative — ambiguous inputs fall to the fallback more often, reducing routing errors but increasing fallback volume. The optimal value is calibrated empirically on a held-out labelled validation set.

attrs.alt: Conditional prompting pipeline: input → condition evaluator → routing diamond → three prompt templates (billing, technical, fallback) → shared LLM → response

content.text: Raw text or structured data enters the pipeline. No transformation at this stage.
A dedicated, lightweight step that classifies or scores the input. It produces a route label and an optional confidence score.
The dispatcher maps the route label to a specific prompt template. This logic lives in code (a Python
statement or dictionary lookup), never inside the prompt.
Each branch has its own system prompt and optional user-message transformation. Templates are parameterised with named placeholders.
All branches converge on the same model instance (though model selection can also be conditional). The prompt determines behaviour; the model is stateless between calls.
The LLM response is either the final answer, input to the next chain step, or a tool call that triggers further downstream action.

Architectural principle: the model is the worker; the router is the manager. Keep routing logic as deterministic and minimal as possible. Push complexity into the prompt templates, not the routing code, so every branch stays independently inspectable and testable.
The system receives inputs from multiple distinct user intents that require different personas, constraints, or output formats.
Different user tiers (free vs. paid, UK vs. EU) must receive responses built from different compliance constraints or data-access rules.
Detecting the input language and routing to a language-specific template avoids the quality degradation of asking one prompt to handle all languages.
After generation, a validator evaluates quality and conditionally routes to a revision prompt if output falls below threshold.
Routing short, simple queries to a cheaper model while sending complex analytical queries to a powerful model optimises cost-quality tradeoff dynamically.
Poor Fits — When Not to Use
Fewer than two meaningfully different behaviours.
A single well-written system prompt is simpler and easier to maintain. Introduce conditionals only when they solve a concrete quality or cost problem.
If the classifier is unreliable on your input distribution, routing errors hurt overall quality more than branch specialisation helps.
Premature conditional architecture adds overhead before the actual input distribution is understood. Start monolithic; refactor once patterns emerge.
Hard real-time budgets below evaluator latency.
If the SLA is under 200 ms and the evaluator costs 150 ms, only a rule-based (zero-LLM) evaluator can fit.
Collect 50–200 real examples of system inputs and label each with its intended behaviour. Cluster similar labels into
. Aim for no more than 5–7 routes before adding a fallback; more routes increase both maintenance burden and evaluator error rate.

Select a rule-based, LLM-classifier, or embedding-similarity evaluator based on latency budget and input structure. Build and test the evaluator in isolation against a labelled dataset before integrating it into the pipeline.
For each route, write a focused system prompt containing only the instructions relevant to that route. Use named placeholders (e.g.
Step 4 — Build the Dispatcher in Code

Implement routing logic in code, not inside a prompt. A Python dictionary mapping route labels to template strings is the most readable form. Ensure every possible evaluator output has a mapped template, including the fallback.

Run the full pipeline against a labelled test set. Track evaluator accuracy (correctly routed %), per-route generation quality, and end-to-end latency. Log the route label for every production request. A sudden spike in the fallback rate often means a new user intent has emerged that the route taxonomy doesn't cover.

# Minimal conditional prompting pipeline — Python + Anthropic SDK
# pip install anthropic
import json
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()   # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

# ── 1. Prompt templates (one per intent) ─────────────────────────────────
TEMPLATES = {
    "billing": (
        "You are a billing specialist. Be empathetic, reference specific "
        "amounts and dates, and escalate refunds above $500."
    ),
    "technical": (
        "You are a senior engineer. Give concise, actionable debug steps "
        "and include code snippets where helpful."
    ),
    "general": (
        "You are a friendly assistant. Answer briefly and, if the topic "
        "belongs to billing or technical support, offer to transfer."
    ),
    "fallback": (
        "You are an assistant. The request is unclear. "
        "Ask ONE specific clarifying question — do not attempt an answer yet."
    ),
}

# ── 2. Evaluator: cheap LLM classifier ───────────────────────────────────
CLASSIFIER_PROMPT = """
Classify the message into one of: billing, technical, general.
Reply with JSON only: {"category": "<label>", "confidence": 0.0-1.0}
- billing: invoices, charges, refunds, subscriptions
- technical: code, APIs, errors, integrations
- general: everything else
"""

CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD = 0.75

def classify(message: str) -> tuple[str, float]:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=64,
        messages=[{"role": "user",
                   "content": f"{CLASSIFIER_PROMPT}\n\nMessage: {message}"}],
    )
    result = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)
    return result.get("category", "general"), float(result.get("confidence", 0))

# ── 3. Dispatcher + generator ─────────────────────────────────────────────
def respond(message: str) -> dict:
    route, conf = classify(message)
    if conf < CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD:
        route = "fallback"

    system = TEMPLATES[route]
    gen = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": message}],
    )
    return {"route": route, "confidence": conf, "reply": gen.content[0].text}

# ── Demo ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    tests = [
        "Why was I charged twice last month?",
        "I'm getting a 401 on the /v1/messages endpoint.",
        "What does the Pro plan include?",
    ]
    for msg in tests:
        r = respond(msg)
        print(f"[{r['route']} / conf={r['confidence']:.2f}] {r['reply'][:120]}...")

In this pattern, a validator evaluates the LLM's own output and conditionally routes to a revision prompt if quality is insufficient. The loop exits only when the output passes or the maximum iteration count is reached.

# Output-driven conditional: quality-gate loop
# The validator checks the draft; if it fails, the system routes to revision.
import json
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

DRAFT_SYS = "You are a technical writer. Write a clear 100-150 word explanation of: {topic}"

QUALITY_PROMPT = """
Rate this explanation on accuracy, clarity, completeness.
Reply with JSON only: {{"passes": bool, "issues": ["..."]}}
Text: {text}
"""

REVISION_SYS = """
You are a technical editor. Fix the issues listed below in the draft.
Issues: {issues}
Draft: {draft}
Return only the revised text.
"""

def generate_with_gate(topic: str, max_rounds: int = 3) -> str:
    draft = None
    for round_num in range(1, max_rounds + 1):
        if draft is None:
            prompt = DRAFT_SYS.format(topic=topic)
            user_msg = f"Explain: {topic}"
        else:
            prompt = REVISION_SYS.format(issues=", ".join(issues), draft=draft)
            user_msg = "Revise the draft."

        r = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5", max_tokens=400,
            system=prompt, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}],
        )
        draft = r.content[0].text

        # ── Conditional branch: evaluate quality ─────────────────────────
        q = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=128,
            messages=[{"role": "user",
                       "content": QUALITY_PROMPT.format(text=draft)}],
        )
        verdict = json.loads(q.content[0].text)
        issues = verdict.get("issues", [])
        print(f"Round {round_num}: passes={verdict['passes']}, issues={issues}")
        if verdict["passes"]:
            break           # ← conditional exit: no revision needed

    return draft

print(generate_with_gate("vector embeddings in semantic search"))

For structured inputs such as webhook payloads, a rule-based router costs nothing at evaluation time and is fully deterministic. This JavaScript example routes payment and fraud events to specialised prompts:

A mid-size SaaS company processes roughly 8,000 inbound support messages per day across billing, technical integration, and general account queries. Their production system uses a two-tier router: a regex fast path (O(1), free) handles the majority of clearly labelled messages; ambiguous messages escalate to an LLM classifier. This hybrid cuts classifier LLM calls by roughly 60% compared to routing every message through the LLM.
Constraints: p95 latency target of 3 s end-to-end, four supported languages (EN, FR, DE, ES), three intent routes (billing 28%, technical 51%, general 21%). The evaluator uses
. Messages over 2,000 characters run a condensation sub-step first to extract the core question before classification.
Mistake 1 — Routing Logic Inside the Prompt

Asking the main LLM to decide how to respond based on inline conditionals in a monolithic system prompt conflates evaluation and generation. This reduces quality of both, makes per-branch iteration impossible, and produces no loggable routing signal.

# WRONG — routing buried inside one large prompt
BAD_SYSTEM = """
You are a support assistant.
If the user asks about billing, respond as a billing expert.
If the user asks about code, respond as an engineer.
...
"""
# Problems: instruction conflicts, no per-branch testing, full token cost on every request.

# RIGHT — routing in code, focused template per route
def respond(msg):
    route, _ = classify(msg)          # explicit evaluation step
    system = TEMPLATES[route]         # route-specific prompt
    return call_llm(system, msg)      # clean generation

# WRONG — blindly trusting the top prediction
route, confidence = classify(message)
template = TEMPLATES[route]   # routes even when confidence = 0.31

# RIGHT — threshold gate
THRESHOLD = 0.75
route, confidence = classify(message)
if confidence < THRESHOLD:
    route = "fallback"        # ask for clarification instead
template = TEMPLATES[route]

A fallback that simply says "I don't understand" frustrates users and provides no path forward. The fallback prompt should extract useful signal for the next turn by asking one specific clarifying question and offering the available route categories.

Building a six-branch conditional pipeline before validating that the product has six distinct user needs is premature. Most systems should start with a single prompt and introduce their first conditional split only when a concrete quality or cost problem appears in production data.
Record the route label, confidence score, and evaluator model for every request. This is the primary diagnostic signal for the entire pipeline.
Build a labelled test set of at least 100 examples per route. Track precision and recall per route, not just aggregate accuracy.
A template that handles two distinct sub-cases signals the need for another route or a nested sub-router. Each template should have exactly one job.
Use a smaller model for the evaluator.
The classifier call is latency-sensitive and cost-sensitive. A small model is often as accurate as a large one for classification tasks at 10–20× lower cost.
Store templates in source control with semantic versioning. Roll back to a previous version on regression, exactly as with application code.
Monitor route distribution as a leading indicator.
A sudden increase in fallback rate or anomalous spike in one route often precedes user complaints by hours. Treat routing metrics as a health signal.
A two-stage conditional pipeline adds one full LLM round-trip versus a single-stage pipeline. Typical latency breakdown at intermediate throughput (using Haiku as evaluator, Sonnet as generator):
If the input has a predictable structure (event type field, command prefix), a rule-based evaluator eliminates the classification round-trip entirely.
Template strings are static. Cache the filled prompt in memory after the first variable substitution for a given (route, user) combination.
Use prompt caching for large system prompts.
Anthropic's prompt caching feature can cache the static system prompt prefix, reducing input token cost by up to 90% and TTFT by 85% on repeated requests to the same branch.
If a pipeline fans out to multiple branches that do not depend on each other (e.g. scoring multiple criteria simultaneously), run them concurrently with
Several frameworks offer first-class support for conditional prompting patterns:
Declarative conditional routing that composes with LCEL chains. Branches are expressed as
Routes queries to specialised sub-engines (e.g. vector store, SQL engine, keyword search) based on LLM or embedding classification.
modules can implement conditional dispatch as part of compiled, optimised pipelines where the evaluator is tuned alongside the generator.
Visual pipeline builder with a built-in condition node that routes on LLM output, JSON field values, or schema validation results.
The following snippet shows a LangChain declarative conditional pipeline with a lightweight classifier routing to specialist chains:

# Using LangChain to build a conditional prompting pipeline declaratively
# pip install langchain langchain-anthropic

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableBranch, RunnableLambda

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

# Classifier chain
classifier_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Classify as billing|technical|general. Reply with one word.\n\nMessage: {input}"
)
classifier = classifier_prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()

# Specialist chains
billing_chain  = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "You are a billing specialist. Answer: {input}"
) | ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-5") | StrOutputParser()

technical_chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "You are a senior engineer. Answer: {input}"
) | ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-5") | StrOutputParser()

general_chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "You are a support agent. Answer briefly: {input}"
) | llm | StrOutputParser()

# RunnableBranch wires the conditional logic declaratively
branch = RunnableBranch(
    (lambda x: "billing"   in x["topic"], billing_chain),
    (lambda x: "technical" in x["topic"], technical_chain),
    general_chain,
)

full_chain = RunnableLambda(
    lambda msg: {"input": msg, "topic": classifier.invoke({"input": msg})}
) | branch

print(full_chain.invoke("Why was I charged twice in June?"))

An adversary can craft inputs designed to manipulate the evaluator into routing to an unintended template — for example, including classifier-baiting phrases in a malicious query to gain access to a privileged prompt branch. Mitigations: validate the route label against an allowlist, never embed the raw user message inside the classification result, and log and alert on unusual route distributions.

If the evaluator depends on an LLM, its availability becomes a dependency for every request. Mitigations: implement a rule-based fast path as a circuit breaker; if the evaluator call times out or returns an unparseable response, fall back to the most conservative route (typically
) rather than erroring the entire request.

When compliance requirements change, every affected branch must be updated. Without a centralised template registry and per-branch automated regression tests, branches drift out of sync. Treat each template update as a deployment event: stage, test, and roll out with rollback capability.
Always validate the evaluator output format.
Parse the JSON response defensively; default to fallback on any parse error.
Prevent one branch from consuming unexpectedly large token counts that would exceed rate limits or cost budgets for other users.
Branches that access privileged data (account details, PII) require more rigorous testing and separate access controls.
An evaluator classifies the input; a dispatcher selects the matching prompt template. Keep these two phases separate and explicit.
The router belongs in code, not in a prompt.
Routing logic implemented as a dictionary or match statement is deterministic, loggable, and testable — none of which is true for instructions buried in a monolithic system prompt.
Choose the cheapest evaluator that meets accuracy requirements.
Regex beats LLM for structured inputs. Small models beat large models for well-defined classification. Reserve powerful models for generation.
Always gate routing on a minimum confidence score. Low-confidence inputs routed to the fallback produce better outcomes than misrouted inputs that receive authoritative but wrong responses.
The fallback branch deserves as much design effort as primary routes.
It handles unknown unknowns and is the system's safety net. An under-designed fallback fails exactly when the system most needs a guardrail.
Conditional prompting is a refactoring target, not a starting point.
Begin with a single prompt. Introduce the first conditional split only when concrete quality or cost problems emerge from real production data.
Monitor route distribution as a health signal.
Spikes in fallback rate or anomalous shifts in route mix are early indicators of a new user intent, a classifier regression, or an adversarial probe.
